title: Time Series Forcasting with TCN
text: This post introduce multi-variates time-series forecasting using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs). Multivariates time seriesMultivariate time series exists in many real world applications, for example, healthcare, financial marketing, IoT. Usually we denote a multivariate time series $\mathbf{X}=\left\{\mathbf{x}_{1}, \mathbf{x}_{2}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_{T}\right\}$ as a sequence of $T$ observations, the $t-t h$ observation $\mathbf{x}_{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$ Usually, the multivariate time-series will in form: feature 1 feature 2 feature 3 … feature M $t1$ $t2$ … $tN$ Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs) What is TCN?TCN = 1D fully-convolutional network + causal dialated convolutions 1D fully-convolutional network(FCN): The FCN is widely used in semantic segmentation tasks, 1D FCN have a structure where each hidden layer is the same size as the input layer. Hence, the model is able to handle the sequence-to-sequence tasks. Causal dialated convolutions: To prevent from leakage by using future information, causal convolutions were applied, where an output at time $t$ is convolved only with elements from time $t$ and earlier in the previous layer. As simple causal convolution is only able to look back at a history with size linear in the depth of the network, when the input is a long sequence, the network have to be quite deep to obtain a receptive field that covers the input. To tackle this challenge, dialated convoluntion was employed, which enable an exponentially large receptive field. Why using TCN? Flexible receptive field size and variable length inputs The receptive field size of a TCN can be changed in multiple ways: making network deeper by stacking more dilated (causal) convolutional layers using larger dilation factors increasing the filter size TCNs thus afford better control of the model’s memory size, and are easy to adapt to different domains. Parallel computations Unlike conventional RNNs, where the predictions for next timesteps must wait for the previous timesteps to complete, convolutions can be done in parallel since the same filter is used in each layer. Therefore, in both training and evaluation, a long input sequence can be processed as a whole in TCN, instead of sequentially as in RNN. Stable gradients Unlike conventional RNNs, the backpropagation path of TCNs different from the time direction of the sequence, thus avoid the problem of exploding/vanishing gradients, which is the major problem of RNNs. Low memory requirement for training As in a TCN the filters are shared across a layer, with the backpropagation path depending only on network depth, the memory usage could significantly reduced, especially in the case of a long input sequence, where LSTMs and GRUs can easily use up a lot of memory to store the partial results for their multiple cell gates. Implement TCN for time-series forecasting tasks in PyTorchData preparationAs TCN is slide over time-axis, we need to prepare our data with a shape of $[N, C, L]$, where $N$ is the batch-size, $C$ is the number of features, $L$ is the sequence length. The time-series now looks: $t1$ $t2$ $t3$ … $tN$ feature 1 feature 2 … feature M Implementation Reference: https://github.com/locuslab/TCN import torch import torch.nn as nn from torch.nn.utils import weight_norm class Chomp1d(nn.Module): def __init__(self, chomp_size): super(Chomp1d, self).__init__() self.chomp_size = chomp_size def forward(self, x): return x[:, :, :-self.chomp_size].contiguous() class TemporalBlock(nn.Module): def __init__(self, n_inputs, n_outputs, kernel_size, stride, dilation, padding, dropout=0.2): super(TemporalBlock, self).__init__() self.conv1 = weight_norm(nn.Conv1d(n_inputs, n_outputs, kernel_size, stride=stride, padding=padding, dilation=dilation)) self.chomp1 = Chomp1d(padding) self.relu1 = nn.ReLU() self.dropout1 = nn.Dropout(dropout) self.conv2 = weight_norm(nn.Conv1d(n_outputs, n_outputs, kernel_size, stride=stride, padding=padding, dilation=dilation)) self.chomp2 = Chomp1d(padding) self.relu2 = nn.ReLU() self.dropout2 = nn.Dropout(dropout) self.net = nn.Sequential(self.conv1, self.chomp1, self.relu1, self.dropout1, self.conv2, self.chomp2, self.relu2, self.dropout2) self.downsample = nn.Conv1d(n_inputs, n_outputs, 1) if n_inputs != n_outputs else None self.relu = nn.ReLU() self.init_weights() def init_weights(self): self.conv1.weight.data.normal_(0, 0.01) self.conv2.weight.data.normal_(0, 0.01) if self.downsample is not None: self.downsample.weight.data.normal_(0, 0.01) def forward(self, x): out = self.net(x) res = x if self.downsample is None else self.downsample(x) return self.relu(out + res) class TemporalConvNet(nn.Module): def __init__(self, num_inputs, num_channels, kernel_size=2, dropout=0.2): super(TemporalConvNet, self).__init__() layers = [] num_levels = len(num_channels) for i in range(num_levels): dilation_size = 2 ** i in_channels = num_inputs if i == 0 else num_channels[i-1] out_channels = num_channels[i] layers += [TemporalBlock(in_channels, out_channels, kernel_size, stride=1, dilation=dilation_size, padding=(kernel_size-1) * dilation_size, dropout=dropout)] self.network = nn.Sequential(*layers) def forward(self, x): return self.network(x) When forwarding, if we want output as the same length as the input, set is_sequence=True, or set is_sequence=False if we only need to predict the last time-step. import torch from torch import nn from models.tcn import TemporalConvNet import torch.nn.functional as F class TCNs(nn.Module): def __init__(self, input_size, output_size, num_channels, kernel_size, dropout, is_sequence): super(TCNs, self).__init__() self.tcn = TemporalConvNet(input_size, num_channels, kernel_size, dropout=dropout) self.linear = nn.Linear(num_channels[-1], output_size) self.is_sequence = is_sequence def init_weights(self): self.linear.weight.data.normal_(0, 0.01) def forward(self, inputs): """Inputs have to have dimension (N, C_in, L_in)""" y1 = self.tcn(inputs) # if we want output as the same length as the input if self.is_sequence: o = self.linear(y1.transpose(1, 2)) # if we only want the output from the last time step else: o = self.linear(y1[:, :, -1]) return o Reference Bai, Shaojie, J. Zico Kolter, and Vladlen Koltun. “An empirical evaluation of generic convolutional and recurrent networks for sequence modeling.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1803.01271 (2018).

title: Support Vector Machine
text: An throughout introduction to Support Vector Machine. IntroductionSupport Vector Machine is a very famous classification algorithm ( can be used in regression challenges as well ) in Machine Learning, which occupied the dominant position of classification algorithms before Deep Learning. Even today, there are still many cases where SVM will be used. This article will explain SVM in detail, and in the end I will show the MATLAB codes for implementing different SVMs, which can help you to have a deeper understanding on how SVM works. What is SVMSVM is a kind of supervised machine learning algorithm, which usually used for two-classes classification. There are 6 key parts in SVM: Margins Primal Problem Lagrange Dual Problem Soft Margin Kernel Before we go into details, let’s start with figuring out what exactly SVM is doing. Let’s consider a scenario, ChewChew needs to apply for a loan from the bank to buy a house. The bank needs to decide whether to approve ChewChew’s application. Usually, the bank decided by age-income, and the bank had the following historical data: What should bank do if they want make the decision based on historical data? We may naturally want to draw a line to divide the historical data into two parts, if ChewChew drop in the upper half part, then approve his application, else rejection. The line we draw also called Hyperplane In geometry, a hyperplane is a subspace whose dimension is one less than that of its ambient space. If a space is 3-dimensional then its hyperplanes are the 2-dimensional planes, while if the space is 2-dimensional, its hyperplanes are the 1-dimensional lines.One Hyperplane can be denoted by $g(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}+b=0$ This Hyperplane will classify a given data \mathbf{x}_{i} : \begin{equation} \operatorname{sgn}\left[g\left(\mathbf{x}_{i}\right)\right]=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} {+1} & {\text { if }} & {g\left(\mathbf{x}_{i}\right)>0} \\ {-1} & {\text { if }} & {g\left(\mathbf{x}_{i}\right)
